stem: What real number is excluded from the (largest possible) domain of \( f(x) = \dfrac{x^{2} + 1}{[[a]]x + ([[b]])} \)?
solution: A rational function is undefined only where its denominator is zero. Solving \( [[a]]x + ([[b]]) = 0 \) gives \( x = [[answer]] \); the numerator \( x^{2}+1 \) is never zero, so this is the only excluded value.
distractor_rationales: This forgets the negative sign when solving \( [[a]]x + ([[b]]) = 0 \).
This inverts the fraction, dividing \( a \) by \( b \) instead of solving for \( x \).
This forgets to divide by the leading coefficient \( [[a]] \).
This both forgets the sign and forgets to divide by \( [[a]] \).
This is off by one from the excluded value.
This is off by one from the excluded value.

stem: What is the smallest real number in the (largest possible) domain of \( f(x) = \sqrt{[[a]]x + ([[b]])} \)?
solution: A square root requires a non-negative radicand: \( [[a]]x + ([[b]]) \ge 0 \), i.e. \( x \ge \dfrac{-([[b]])}{[[a]]} \). The smallest allowed value, where the radicand is exactly zero, is \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This flips the sign of the boundary value.
This forgets the negative sign when isolating \( x \).
This inverts the fraction, dividing \( a \) by \( b \) instead of solving for \( x \).
This reports the constant term directly instead of solving for the boundary.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The (largest possible) domain of \( f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x^{2} + ([[B]])x + ([[C]])} \) excludes exactly two real numbers. What is their product?
solution: The excluded values are the roots of \( x^{2} + ([[B]])x + ([[C]]) = 0 \). By Vieta's formulas, the product of the roots of a monic quadratic \( x^{2}+Bx+C \) is \( C \), so the product of the excluded values is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the linear coefficient \( B \) directly instead of the product of the roots.
This gives the sum of the roots (negated), not their product.
This squares the constant term instead of using it directly as the product.
This multiplies the two coefficients together, which has no basis in Vieta's formulas.
This is off by one from the correct product.
This is off by one from the correct product.

stem: The (largest possible) domain of \( f(x) = \ln([[a]]x + ([[b]])) \) is an interval \( (k, \infty) \), where \( k \) itself is NOT in the domain. What is \( k \)?
solution: A logarithm requires a strictly positive argument: \( [[a]]x + ([[b]]) > 0 \), i.e. \( x > \dfrac{-([[b]])}{[[a]]} \). So the domain is \( (k, \infty) \) with \( k = [[answer]] \), and \( k \) itself is excluded since the inequality is strict.
distractor_rationales: This flips the sign of the boundary value.
This forgets the negative sign when isolating \( x \).
This reports the constant term directly instead of solving for the boundary.
This negates the constant term instead of solving the full equation for \( x \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: What is the smallest integer in the (largest possible) domain of \( f(x) = \sqrt{[[a]]x + ([[b]])} \)?
solution: The domain requires \( [[a]]x + ([[b]]) \ge 0 \), i.e. \( x \ge \dfrac{-([[b]])}{[[a]]} \). Since this boundary is not an integer, the smallest integer in the domain is the next integer up: \( \left\lceil \dfrac{-([[b]])}{[[a]]} \right\rceil = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This rounds down instead of up, giving an integer that does not satisfy the inequality.
This forgets the negative sign before rounding.
This reports the exact (non-integer) boundary rather than rounding to the smallest valid integer.
This forgets the negative sign entirely.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The (largest possible) domain of \( f(x) = \dfrac{\sqrt{x - ([[a]])}}{x - ([[b]])} \) is the interval \( [\,[[a]],\, \infty) \) with exactly one additional point excluded (where the denominator vanishes). What is that excluded point?
solution: The square root requires \( x \ge [[a]] \), and the denominator requires \( x \ne [[b]] \). Since \( [[b]] > [[a]] \), the point \( x = [[b]] \) lies inside \( [[[a]], \infty) \) and must be separately excluded.
distractor_rationales: This is the left endpoint of the square-root restriction, which is included, not excluded.
This adds the two boundary values together, which has no meaning here.
This computes the gap between the two boundary values instead of the excluded point itself.
This negates the correct excluded point.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
